text: The surgeon retracts the liver using a robotic arm, providing exposure to the triangle of Calot. The gallbladder appears mildly inflamed. The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the triangle, focusing on the infundibulum and neck of the gallbladder. Firefly imaging is used to identify ductal structures, but the cystic duct does not illuminate due to occlusion.

The surgeon practices the open book concept by dissecting the medial attachments of the gallbladder from the liver, enhancing exposure of the triangle of K-low. The clip applier is used as a retractor to facilitate this separation. As the dissection progresses, the surgeon encounters a larger vessel and decides to leave it undisturbed due to uncertainty about the anatomy. The lateral attachments are also taken down to further separate the gallbladder from the liver, improving exposure of the critical area.

The surgeon achieves excellent exposure of the triangle of KLO, pulling the gallbladder away from the liver to minimize the risk of injuring significant structures. Using the Firefly imaging system, the common hepatic duct, common bile duct, and green cystic duct are visible. A cystic duct stone is identified between the cystic duct and gallbladder, preventing ICG uptake. The surgeon plans to dissect the cystic duct section containing the stone and clip and divide it between the stone and common bile duct. The right hepatic artery is visible, with the cystic duct branching off from it.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct coming off the right hepatic artery and clips it to stop bleeding. Using bipolar cautery, the gallbladder side is divided with a hook. The surgeon carefully lifts thin tissue, checking with Firefly to avoid any aberrant ducts joining the cystic duct where the stone is located. The open book technique aids in the dissection by providing clear exposure, allowing the surgeon to work around potential structures behind the artery while maintaining distance from the liver.

The surgeon carefully dissects small sections of tissue near the area of potential aberrant duct using Firefly imaging to check for occlusions. The thickened tissue is due to inflammation from cystic obstruction. The right hepatic artery is visible, with a small cystic duct or artery branching from it.

The surgeon clips and divides the cystic duct, ensuring a sufficient length is skeletonized while maintaining proximity to the common bile duct. Firefly imaging is used to verify the anatomical structures during the procedure.

The surgeon assesses the lighting on the cystic duct, opting to use scissors instead of the hook cautery due to insufficient illumination. While waiting for the scissors, more of the gallbladder is removed to improve efficiency. The back wall of the gallbladder appears inflamed due to chronic cholecystitis and cystic duct occlusion. The surgery concludes with the use of Firefly to check for bile leaks, followed by the introduction of an EndoCatch bag through the umbilical port for specimen removal.
